prompt: Why is a 'big-bang' switch (turning off the old system and turning on the new one all at once) considered risky?
choices: It concentrates every possible failure into a single moment with no easy way back
It always costs more money than an incremental migration
It requires writing more code than any other approach
It is impossible to test the new system at all

prompt: Which of the following are hallmarks of a safe, incremental migration? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The system keeps working after every individual step
There is a fast rollback available at each stage
Traffic is cut over gradually rather than all at once
The old system is deleted first, before the new one is verified

prompt: What does it mean to 'find the seams' before a migration?
choices: Locate the points where your code talks to the dependency, ideally routing them through one thin layer
Delete all old data so the new system starts clean
Schedule the migration for a weekend to reduce traffic
Rewrite the entire application from scratch

prompt: In a data-store migration, what is the purpose of 'dual-writing' (writing to both the old and new store for a period)?
choices: To permanently keep two copies of every system forever
To keep the new store's data current and consistent so it can be verified before reads switch to it
To make the migration finish faster by skipping verification
To delete the old store immediately after the first write
